title: Diabetic Retinopathy
oneLiner: A microvascular complication of diabetes causing progressive retinal ischemia, neovascularisation, and vision loss—the leading cause of blindness among working-age adults.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 55-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 12 years, HbA1c persistently 8.5–9.5% (69–80 mmol/mol), presents for her annual diabetic eye screening. She has no visual symptoms—no blurring, floaters, or pain. On fundoscopy, you see multiple dot-blot haemorrhages, hard exudates in a circinate pattern, and a few cotton-wool spots in the posterior pole. The macula appears thickened. There are no new vessels. You diagnose non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy (NPDR) with moderate severity and refer for OCT.

**Dissect the vignette – pattern clues:**
- **No symptoms** → Early DR is asymptomatic; screening is essential. Symptom onset often signifies advanced disease (PDR or macular oedema).
- **Long diabetes duration (>10 years)** → Duration is the strongest risk factor for DR (more than HbA1c at any single point).
- **Persistently high HbA1c** → Chronic hyperglycaemia drives retinal damage; acute fluctuations less predictive.
- **Dot-blot haemorrhages** → Ruptured microaneurysms in the deep capillary plexus.
- **Hard exudates (circinate)** → Lipid leakage from damaged capillaries; indicates chronic oedema.
- **Cotton-wool spots** → Nerve fibre layer infarcts (ischaemia) – marker of capillary non-perfusion.
- **Macular thickening** → Suggests diabetic macular oedema (DMO) – most common cause of vision loss in NPDR.
- **No new vessels** → Rules out proliferative disease (PDR); management focuses on risk factor control and DMO.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
1. **“Rapid onset” in a newly diagnosed type 1 diabetic** – Can occur in “early worsening” after insulin therapy (transient, usually mild). Rarely, presentation with florid PDR and vitreous haemorrhage in a teenage type 1 patient with poor control.
2. **Sudden painless vision loss in a patient with known NPDR** – Think vitreous haemorrhage from occult new vessels (PDR) or tractional retinal detachment. Often the patient denies floaters earlier—because vitreous detachment may be silent.
3. **Blind spot or field defect without fundus changes** – Could be diabetic papillopathy (temporary optic disc swelling) or non-ischaemic central retinal vein occlusion mimicking DR. Always check IOP and perform OCT of the disc.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
Diabetic retinopathy (DR) is damage to the blood vessels of the retina caused by long-term high blood sugar. It starts with leakage (dot/blot haemorrhages, exudates) and progresses to vessel closure (cotton-wool spots, capillary dropout). Eventually, the retina is starved of oxygen and sends out signals to grow abnormal new vessels (proliferative DR), which are fragile and bleed easily.

**Why this topic matters:**
- **Frequency:** DR affects ~1 in 3 people with diabetes. It’s the most common cause of preventable blindness in adults aged 20–65.
- **Stakes:** Vision loss from DR is largely preventable with screening and early treatment. Once vitreous haemorrhage or tractional detachment occurs, outcomes are worse even with surgery.
- **Common pitfalls:** Junior doctors often dismiss “background DR” as benign—but even mild NPDR can progress rapidly if glycaemic control worsens. Always check for macular oedema on OCT (clinical exam alone misses 40% of DMO). Most screening programmes refer based on grading, but your role is to ensure proper follow-up and risk factor optimisation.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story of development:**

1. **Hyperglycaemia → biochemical injury** – High intracellular glucose leads to sorbitol accumulation, advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), and oxidative stress. These damage capillary pericytes and endothelial cells.

2. **Pericyte loss & capillary basement membrane thickening** – Pericytes normally provide structural support and regulate blood flow. Their loss weakens capillary walls → microaneurysm formation (the earliest clinical sign). Basement membrane thickening reduces luminal diameter → impaired perfusion.

3. **Capillary leakage** – Weakened vessel walls leak plasma proteins and lipids. On fundoscopy: **hard exudates** (lipid deposits) and **dot-blot haemorrhages** (intraretinal blood). This is the non-proliferative stage.

4. **Capillary closure / ischaemia** – Progressive occlusion of capillaries leads to areas of non-perfusion. The retina tries to compensate:
   - **Cotton-wool spots** – nerve fibre layer infarcts (axonal stasis) – **why?** Ischaemia of the NFL.
   - **Venous beading / looping** – attempts at collateralisation.
   - **Intraretinal microvascular abnormalities (IRMA)** – dilated, tortuous capillaries within the retina that skip the normal capillary bed (shunts). IRMA are a **marker of severe ischaemia** and a harbinger of proliferative disease.

5. **Hypoxia → VEGF upregulation** – When ischaemia becomes severe, the retina releases vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF). This stimulates:
   - **Neovascularisation** – new, fragile vessels that grow on the disc (NVD) or elsewhere (NVE). These lack pericytes and leak easily → vitreous haemorrhage.
   - **Fibrovascular proliferation** – fibrous tissue accompanies new vessels, leading to traction on the retina → **tractional retinal detachment**.
   - **Neovascular glaucoma** – if new vessels grow on the iris (NVI) or into the angle, causing acute angle closure.
   - **Diabetic macular oedema (DMO)** – VEGF also increases vascular permeability at the macula. Fluid accumulation in the inner/outer plexiform layers → vision loss.

**Connect pathophys to pattern cues:**
- **Dot/blot haemorrhages** = microaneurysm rupture (NPDR).  
- **Hard exudates** = chronic lipid leakage (NPDR with macular oedema).  
- **Cotton-wool spots** = ischaemia (nerve fibre layer infarcts).  
- **New vessels** = VEGF-driven angiogenesis (PDR).  
- **Vitreous haemorrhage** = bleeding from fragile new vessels.  
- **Macular thickening** = fluid from VEGF + other cytokines.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Metabolic (most common)** | Chronic hyperglycaemia (high HbA1c, especially >7.5%/58 mmol/mol) | “Uncontrolled diabetes for 10+ years” |
| **Duration of diabetes** | Risk increases linearly after 10 years for type 2, 5 years for type 1 | “Diagnosed at age 20, now 35” |
| **Hypertension** | Synergistic with hyperglycaemia; accelerates capillary damage | “BP 160/90 despite two drugs” |
| **Dyslipidaemia** | High LDL and triglycerides contribute to hard exudates and macular oedema | “Total cholesterol 7.2 mmol/L” |
| **Nephropathy** | Diabetic kidney disease → albuminuria → worse DR (shared microvascular disease) | “eGFR 40, urine ACR 300” |
| **Pregnancy** | Hormonal changes + increased blood volume can worsen pre-existing DR | “Pregnant woman with type 1 diabetes, retinopathy stable prior” |
| **Rapid glycaemic correction** | “Early worsening” phenomenon when HbA1c drops >2% in 3 months (risk of transient NPDR progression) | “Started insulin pump 3 months ago, now BG under 7 all day” |
| **Obesity / insulin resistance** | Linked via inflammatory cytokines and endothelial dysfunction | “BMI 38, metabolic syndrome” |
| **Smoking** | Vasoconstriction + oxidative stress | “Smokes 20/day, 30 pack-years” |

**Most common cause:** Chronic hyperglycaemia in a patient with diabetes duration >10 years. No other cause is needed—DR is the retinal signature of sustained glucose toxicity.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
The widely used **International Clinical Diabetic Retinopathy Disease Severity Scale** has five grades:
- No apparent retinopathy
- Mild NPDR (≥1 microaneurysm only)
- Moderate NPDR (more than mild, less than severe)
- Severe NPDR (any of: 20+ dot/blot haemorrhages per quadrant, venous beading in ≥2 quadrants, IRMA in ≥1 quadrant) – the “4-2-1 rule”
- Proliferative DR (NVD, NVE, vitreous or pre-retinal haemorrhage)

For DMO, the **Macular Edema Grade** is separate:  
- Mild DMO (some retinal thickening or exudates away from fovea)  
- Moderate DMO (thickening/exudates near fovea)  
- Severe DMO (thickening involving fovea)

**Pattern-recognition – single finding that confirms/rules out:**
- **New vessels on the disc (NVD)** → confirms PDR (needs urgent ophthalmology)
- **No microaneurysms** on fundoscopy in a diabetic → virtually rules out DR (unless very early NPDR with undetectable MA)

**Test-by-test interpretation:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Dilated fundoscopy** | Direct visualisation of retina, disc, vessels | Screening and grading | See grading above. Cotton-wool spots = ischaemia; IRMA = severe NPDR |
| **Colour fundus photography** | Standardised images | Record baseline, monitor progression | Same as fundoscopy but objective, allows later comparison |
| **Optical coherence tomography (OCT)** | Cross-section of macula – measures retinal thickness, detects fluid | Gold standard for diagnosing DMO | Central subfield thickness >250 µm (or >2 SD above normal) = DMO. Also shows intraretinal and subretinal fluid |
| **Fluorescein angiography (FFA)** | Dynamic view of retinal circulation: leakage, ischaemic areas, new vessels | Assess ischaemia in severe NPDR/PDR; guide laser | Areas of capillary non-perfusion (dark patches) = ischaemia. Leakage from microaneurysms or new vessels |
| **OCT angiography (OCTA)** | Non-invasive, depth-resolved capillary map | Alternative to FFA; shows microaneurysms and non-perfusion | No need for dye; better visualisation of deep capillary plexus |
| **HbA1c** | 2–3 month glycaemic control | Assess modifiable risk factor | Target <7% (53 mmol/mol) for most; but avoid rapid drops |
| **Blood pressure** | Systolic/diastolic | Assess and treat | Target <130/80 mmHg in diabetes |
| **eGFR and urine ACR** | Kidney function | Screen for nephropathy (strongly associated) | If ACR >3 mg/mmol or eGFR <60, higher risk of DR progression |
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**  
This applies if you suspect **acute vision-threatening DR** (e.g., sudden visual loss):
1. **Assess visual acuity** and check for relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) – indicates severe ischaemia or retinal detachment.
2. **Check IOP** – neovascular glaucoma can cause high IOP (pain, red eye, cloudy cornea, fixed mid-dilated pupil). If IOP >30 mmHg → urgent ophthalmology referral; may need topical beta-blockers + acetazolamide.
3. **Order B-scan ultrasound** if fundus view is poor (e.g., vitreous haemorrhage). B-scan can confirm retinal detachment or vitreous haemorrhage.
4. **Do NOT dilate if there is any suspicion of neovascular glaucoma** (pain, vomiting, cloudy cornea) – angle closure is contraindication to dilation until IOP controlled.
5. **Immediate ophthalmology referral** for any new PDR, vitreous haemorrhage, or DMO affecting central vision.

**Ward / clinic (24–72 hours):**
1. **Optimise modifiable risk factors:**
   - Glycaemic control: aim for HbA1c <7% but avoid rapid correction (drop <2% per 3 months) – risk of early worsening.
   - BP: target <130/80; start ACEi/ARB (e.g., ramipril 2.5 mg OD, titrate).
   - Lipids: atorvastatin 20–40 mg OD (target LDL <1.8 mmol/L).
   - Smoking cessation advice + nicotine replacement if needed.
2. **Ophthalmology team management:**
   - **Diabetic macular oedema (DMO):** First-line = intravitreal anti-VEGF (ranibizumab 0.5 mg, aflibercept 2 mg, or bevacizumab 1.25 mg). Usually monthly injections for 3–6 months then PRN. If anti-VEGF contraindicated, consider focal/grid laser (less effective).
   - **Proliferative DR:** Panretinal photocoagulation (PRP) – laser burns to peripheral retina to reduce ischaemic drive and regress new vessels. Usually 3–4 sessions.
   - **Vitreous haemorrhage:** If mild, observe for 4–6 weeks; if no clearing, vitrectomy. If severe or with retinal detachment, urgent vitrectomy.
3. **Referral to diabetes team** for structured education and insulin optimisation if needed.

**Long-term:**
1. **Annual retinal screening** – if stable NPDR, repeat per local protocol (typically 12 months). If moderate NPDR, 6–12 monthly.
2. **Continuous risk factor management** – this is where you, as the junior doctor, have the biggest impact. Every clinic visit: check HbA1c, BP, lipids, smoking status.
3. **Aspirin / antiplatelets** – no specific contraindication in DR, but not protective. The ADVANCE and ETDRS trials show no benefit for retinopathy progression.
4. **Avoid:** Use of sildenafil/tadalafil can worsen PDR? Actually, in patients with active PDR, PDE5 inhibitors can cause mild transient visual disturbances but no evidence of harm. No mandatory avoidance – but counsel about risk.

**What NOT to do:**
- Do **not** stop anticoagulation for vitreous haemorrhage unless life-threatening – bleeding is from new vessels, not anticoagulation. Stopping warfarin may cause stroke. Discuss with haematology.
- Do **not** dilate eyes in a diabetic patient with acute painful red eye and hazy cornea – suspect neovascular glaucoma; wait for IOP assessment.
- Do **not** assume “background DR” is safe – any cotton-wool spot or multiple haemorrhages warrants closer follow-up (moderate NPDR can progress to PDR in 20–30% within 1 year).
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Diabetic Retinopathy (NPDR/PDR) | Hypertensive Retinopathy | Retinal Vein Occlusion (CRVO) | Ocular Ischaemic Syndrome |
|---------|----------------------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Laterality** | Bilateral (asymmetric possible) | Bilateral (symmetric) | Usually unilateral | Unilateral (70%) |
| **Pain** | None (unless neovascular glaucoma) | Headache if hypertensive crisis | Dull ache / pressure (uncommon) | Eye pain + periorbital pain; worse with upright posture |
| **Key fundus findings** | Dot/blot haemorrhages, hard exudates, cotton-wool spots, new vessels | Arteriovenous nicking, silver wiring, flame haemorrhages, disc oedema (malignant HTN) | Flame + dot haemorrhages in all quadrants, “blood and thunder” fundus, optic disc oedema, venous tortuosity | Mid-peripheral blot haemorrhages, retinal artery narrowing, cherry-red spot (late), disc neovascularisation |
| **Macular involvement** | DMO common (exudates, thickening) | Macular star (if hypertensive encephalopathy) | Macular oedema common (leading cause of vision loss in CRVO) | Macular oedema less common; cilioretinal artery sparing |
| **Systemic clues** | Past history of diabetes (type 1/2) | High BP, LVH, renal disease | Atherosclerosis, HTN, glaucoma | Carotid bruit, transient ischaemic attacks, stroke—especially ipsilateral |
| **Confirmatory test** | Fundoscopy + OCT; FFA shows leakage and non-perfusion | BP >140/90; fundoscopic grading (Keith-Wagener) | FFA shows delayed arm-to-retina time (CRVO) + blocked background fluorescence | Carotid duplex: >70% stenosis; intraocular pressure normal, but ocular perfusion pressure low |
| **Key differentiator** | Presence of diabetes + symmetric findings; microaneurysms are earliest sign | No microaneurysms; arteriolar changes dominate | Unilateral, abrupt onset, venous tortuosity, and absent diabetes history (though can coexist) | Carotid disease history; fundus shows mid-peripheral haemorrhages but disc neovascularisation (NVD) can mimic PDR |

**Rule of thumb:** If a diabetic patient presents with unilateral fundus changes, think vein occlusion or ocular ischaemic syndrome. Bilateral symmetric NPDR with microaneurysms = DR.
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Vitreous haemorrhage** | Sudden painless “cobwebs” or “curtain” of vision loss, sometimes with flashes (vitreous detachment). Patient may report “seeing black spots” | Fundoscopy: blood visible through dilated pupil (if dense, no view). B-scan: intra-vitreal echoes. RAPD negative unless massive. | Urgent ophthalmology referral. If mild, await spontaneous clearing (weeks). If severe or vision not improving, vitrectomy. Do not give anti-VEGF unless DMO present. |
| **Tractional retinal detachment** | Gradual visual field defect (curtain coming down), often in a patient with known PDR. May follow vitreous haemorrhage | Fundoscopy (if view clear): elevated retina with fibrovascular stalks. B-scan: V-shaped retinal detachment. RAPD often positive if macula off. | Emergency: refer for vitrectomy within 24–48 hours if macula threatened. If macula already off, still urgent (within 1 week). |
| **Neovascular glaucoma** | Red, painful eye with nausea/vomiting, photophobia, cloudy cornea | IOP >30–50 mmHg, conjunctival injection, corneal oedema, mid-dilated pupil (not responding to light), NVI (new vessels on iris) | Emergency: topical beta-blocker (timolol 0.5% BD), acetazolamide 500 mg IV or PO stat. If NVI present, refer immediately for panretinal photocoagulation + anti-VEGF (intravitreal). |
| **Diabetic macular oedema** | Gradual central vision blur, difficulty reading, distortion (metamorphopsia). Often bilateral but asymmetric | Fundoscopy: hard exudates in a ring (circinate) near fovea; OCT shows thickening and cysts. | Refer for anti-VEGF therapy; same-day if visual acuity compromised. Treat risk factors. |
| **Ischaemic optic neuropathy** | Sudden painless loss of vision (altitudinal field defect) in a diabetic (especially in type 1) | RAPD+, disc oedema (normal or pale), no retinal haemorrhages | This is anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (AION) – associated with diabetes but not DR per se. No specific treatment; differentiate from PDR (no neovascularisation). |

**Late-presentation patterns:**  
A patient presents with **sudden, severe, painless loss of vision** in one eye and fundus view is completely obscured by blood (no red reflex). You diagnose **dense vitreous haemorrhage**. The danger is **occult retinal detachment** – if the macula detaches while you wait for clearance, vision may be permanently lost. **Always order B-scan** to check for retinal detachment. If B-scan shows detachment, refer for urgent vitrectomy.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics**

1. **The “4-2-1” rule for severe NPDR:**  
   – 4 quadrants of ≥20 dot/blot haemorrhages  
   – 2 quadrants of venous beading  
   – 1 quadrant of IRMA (intraretinal microvascular abnormalities)  
   *Any one of these = severe NPDR (requires follow-up <6 months, risk of PDR ~50% in 1 year)*

2. **“ABCDES” of DR prevention:**  
   – **A** (HbA1c – “A” for glucose)  
   – **B** (Blood pressure)  
   – **C** (Cholesterol)  
   – **D** (Duration – not modifiable but risk increases)  
   – **E** (Eye screening annually)  
   – **S** (Smoking cessation)

3. **“PDR = New vessels + haemorrhage”** (use to recall the hallmark features)

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **“No symptoms does not mean no disease”** – DR is silent until advanced. Annual screening is the key. The first symptom in PDR is often vitreous haemorrhage (sudden floaters/blur).  
2. **Hard exudates + macular thickening = DMO** – treat with anti-VEGF. Don’t leave it.  
3. **Cotton-wool spots in a diabetic = ischaemic stress** – warrants closer follow-up (every 6 months). But also consider hypertensive retinopathy if BP elevated.  
4. **One eye worse than the other** – think vein occlusion, carotid disease, or media opacity (cataract). Asymmetric DR can occur, but bilateral symmetric is typical.  
5. **Sudden visual loss in a diabetic with known PDR** – assume vitreous haemorrhage until proven otherwise. B-scan first; if no detachment, observe.  
6. **Don’t forget to check the iris** – if you see new vessels on the iris (NVI) or in the angle (gonioscopy), that patient has neovascular glaucoma risk. Panretinal laser is urgent.  
7. **Rapid improvement in HbA1c can worsen DR** – counsel patients: “Slow and steady wins the race.” Aim for gradual reduction (1% per 3 months). If starting intensive insulin therapy (e.g., pump), perform baseline retinal screening and ophthalmology review.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Dilating a patient with neovascular glaucoma (risk of angle closure).  
- Confirming “no retinopathy” based on undilated fundoscopy (you miss peripheral lesions).  
- Missing macular oedema on clinical exam (OCT is superior).  
- Assuming that a single cotton-wool spot in a diabetic is due to DR – could be HIV retinopathy, TIA, or emboli. Check for hypertension and carotid disease.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| When to escalate | Who to contact | Why | Specific thresholds |
|------------------|----------------|-----|---------------------|
| **New visual symptoms** (floaters, flashes, curtain) in a patient with known DR | Ophthalmology (on-call) | Possible vitreous haemorrhage, retinal tear, or detachment | If visual acuity drops to counting fingers or worse, or if RAPD+ |
| **Acute red painful eye** with cloudy cornea or nausea/vomiting | Ophthalmology (urgent) + call for IOP check | Neovascular glaucoma – vision can be lost in hours | IOP >30 mmHg; treat as emergency |
| **Any new diagnosis of proliferative DR** on screening | Ophthalmology (refer within 1–2 weeks for PRP/anti-VEGF) | High risk of vitreous haemorrhage or retinal detachment | NVD/NVE on any examination |
| **DMO involving fovea** (central subfield thickness >400 µm or VA <6/12) | Ophthalmology (same-day if VA dropped) | Rapid vision loss if untreated | Refer same-week; treat urgently |
| **Systemic deterioration** – diabetic ketoacidosis or hyperosmolar state in a patient with known DR | ICU / medical registrar | Acute metabolic disturbance can worsen retinal perfusion and precipitate macular oedema | If pH <7.2 or glucose >40 mmol/L with ketosis; NEWS2 triggers |
| **NEWS2 ≥7 or any red flag** (hypotension, altered consciousness) | Crash team / ICU | Sepsis, stroke, or other precipitant may cause DR progression | NEWS2 ≥7 triggers immediate OOH immediate review |
| **B-scan shows retinal detachment** (any) | Ophthalmology (emergency) | Risk of permanent vision loss if macula detaches | If macula attached: emergency vitrectomy within 24h. If detached: still urgent. |
| **Asymmetric DR with carotid bruit** | Vascular surgery / stroke team | Ocular ischaemic syndrome may require carotid endarterectomy | If >70% stenosis on duplex, refer for vascular surgery |

**Final thought:** As the junior doctor, you are the gatekeeper. Never ignore a patient’s complaint of visual symptoms, even if their last screening was normal. The retinal signs you miss today can lead to irreversible blindness tomorrow. Document vision, fundoscopy, and IOP in every diabetic admission—it takes

## Diabetic Retinopathy
**One-liner:** A microvascular complication of diabetes causing progressive retinal ischemia, neovascularisation, and vision loss—the leading cause of blindness among working-age adults.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**  
A 55-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 12 years, HbA1c persistently 8.5–9.5% (69–80 mmol/mol), presents for her annual diabetic eye screening. She has no visual symptoms—no blurring, floaters, or pain. On fundoscopy, you see multiple dot-blot haemorrhages, hard exudates in a circinate pattern, and a few cotton-wool spots in the posterior pole. The macula appears thickened. There are no new vessels. You diagnose non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy (NPDR) with moderate severity and refer for OCT.

**Dissect the vignette – pattern clues:**
- **No symptoms** → Early DR is asymptomatic; screening is essential. Symptom onset often signifies advanced disease (PDR or macular oedema).
- **Long diabetes duration (>10 years)** → Duration is the strongest risk factor for DR (more than HbA1c at any single point).
- **Persistently high HbA1c** → Chronic hyperglycaemia drives retinal damage; acute fluctuations less predictive.
- **Dot-blot haemorrhages** → Ruptured microaneurysms in the deep capillary plexus.
- **Hard exudates (circinate)** → Lipid leakage from damaged capillaries; indicates chronic oedema.
- **Cotton-wool spots** → Nerve fibre layer infarcts (ischaemia) – marker of capillary non-perfusion.
- **Macular thickening** → Suggests diabetic macular oedema (DMO) – most common cause of vision loss in NPDR.
- **No new vessels** → Rules out proliferative disease (PDR); management focuses on risk factor control and DMO.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
1. **“Rapid onset” in a newly diagnosed type 1 diabetic** – Can occur in “early worsening” after insulin therapy (transient, usually mild). Rarely, presentation with florid PDR and vitreous haemorrhage in a teenage type 1 patient with poor control.
2. **Sudden painless vision loss in a patient with known NPDR** – Think vitreous haemorrhage from occult new vessels (PDR) or tractional retinal detachment. Often the patient denies floaters earlier—because vitreous detachment may be silent.
3. **Blind spot or field defect without fundus changes** – Could be diabetic papillopathy (temporary optic disc swelling) or non-ischaemic central retinal vein occlusion mimicking DR. Always check IOP and perform OCT of the disc.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
Diabetic retinopathy (DR) is damage to the blood vessels of the retina caused by long-term high blood sugar. It starts with leakage (dot/blot haemorrhages, exudates) and progresses to vessel closure (cotton-wool spots, capillary dropout). Eventually, the retina is starved of oxygen and sends out signals to grow abnormal new vessels (proliferative DR), which are fragile and bleed easily.

**Why this topic matters:**
- **Frequency:** DR affects ~1 in 3 people with diabetes. It’s the most common cause of preventable blindness in adults aged 20–65.
- **Stakes:** Vision loss from DR is largely preventable with screening and early treatment. Once vitreous haemorrhage or tractional detachment occurs, outcomes are worse even with surgery.
- **Common pitfalls:** Junior doctors often dismiss “background DR” as benign—but even mild NPDR can progress rapidly if glycaemic control worsens. Always check for macular oedema on OCT (clinical exam alone misses 40% of DMO). Most screening programmes refer based on grading, but your role is to ensure proper follow-up and risk factor optimisation.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**The story of development:**

1. **Hyperglycaemia → biochemical injury** – High intracellular glucose leads to sorbitol accumulation, advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), and oxidative stress. These damage capillary pericytes and endothelial cells.

2. **Pericyte loss & capillary basement membrane thickening** – Pericytes normally provide structural support and regulate blood flow. Their loss weakens capillary walls → microaneurysm formation (the earliest clinical sign). Basement membrane thickening reduces luminal diameter → impaired perfusion.

3. **Capillary leakage** – Weakened vessel walls leak plasma proteins and lipids. On fundoscopy: **hard exudates** (lipid deposits) and **dot-blot haemorrhages** (intraretinal blood). This is the non-proliferative stage.

4. **Capillary closure / ischaemia** – Progressive occlusion of capillaries leads to areas of non-perfusion. The retina tries to compensate:
   - **Cotton-wool spots** – nerve fibre layer infarcts (axonal stasis) – **why?** Ischaemia of the NFL.
   - **Venous beading / looping** – attempts at collateralisation.
   - **Intraretinal microvascular abnormalities (IRMA)** – dilated, tortuous capillaries within the retina that skip the normal capillary bed (shunts). IRMA are a **marker of severe ischaemia** and a harbinger of proliferative disease.

5. **Hypoxia → VEGF upregulation** – When ischaemia becomes severe, the retina releases vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF). This stimulates:
   - **Neovascularisation** – new, fragile vessels that grow on the disc (NVD) or elsewhere (NVE). These lack pericytes and leak easily → vitreous haemorrhage.
   - **Fibrovascular proliferation** – fibrous tissue accompanies new vessels, leading to traction on the retina → **tractional retinal detachment**.
   - **Neovascular glaucoma** – if new vessels grow on the iris (NVI) or into the angle, causing acute angle closure.
   - **Diabetic macular oedema (DMO)** – VEGF also increases vascular permeability at the macula. Fluid accumulation in the inner/outer plexiform layers → vision loss.

**Connect pathophys to pattern cues:**
- **Dot/blot haemorrhages** = microaneurysm rupture (NPDR).  
- **Hard exudates** = chronic lipid leakage (NPDR with macular oedema).  
- **Cotton-wool spots** = ischaemia (nerve fibre layer infarcts).  
- **New vessels** = VEGF-driven angiogenesis (PDR).  
- **Vitreous haemorrhage** = bleeding from fragile new vessels.  
- **Macular thickening** = fluid from VEGF + other cytokines.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Metabolic (most common)** | Chronic hyperglycaemia (high HbA1c, especially >7.5%/58 mmol/mol) | “Uncontrolled diabetes for 10+ years” |
| **Duration of diabetes** | Risk increases linearly after 10 years for type 2, 5 years for type 1 | “Diagnosed at age 20, now 35” |
| **Hypertension** | Synergistic with hyperglycaemia; accelerates capillary damage | “BP 160/90 despite two drugs” |
| **Dyslipidaemia** | High LDL and triglycerides contribute to hard exudates and macular oedema | “Total cholesterol 7.2 mmol/L” |
| **Nephropathy** | Diabetic kidney disease → albuminuria → worse DR (shared microvascular disease) | “eGFR 40, urine ACR 300” |
| **Pregnancy** | Hormonal changes + increased blood volume can worsen pre-existing DR | “Pregnant woman with type 1 diabetes, retinopathy stable prior” |
| **Rapid glycaemic correction** | “Early worsening” phenomenon when HbA1c drops >2% in 3 months (risk of transient NPDR progression) | “Started insulin pump 3 months ago, now BG under 7 all day” |
| **Obesity / insulin resistance** | Linked via inflammatory cytokines and endothelial dysfunction | “BMI 38, metabolic syndrome” |
| **Smoking** | Vasoconstriction + oxidative stress | “Smokes 20/day, 30 pack-years” |

**Most common cause:** Chronic hyperglycaemia in a patient with diabetes duration >10 years. No other cause is needed—DR is the retinal signature of sustained glucose toxicity.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**  
The widely used **International Clinical Diabetic Retinopathy Disease Severity Scale** has five grades:
- No apparent retinopathy
- Mild NPDR (≥1 microaneurysm only)
- Moderate NPDR (more than mild, less than severe)
- Severe NPDR (any of: 20+ dot/blot haemorrhages per quadrant, venous beading in ≥2 quadrants, IRMA in ≥1 quadrant) – the “4-2-1 rule”
- Proliferative DR (NVD, NVE, vitreous or pre-retinal haemorrhage)

For DMO, the **Macular Edema Grade** is separate:  
- Mild DMO (some retinal thickening or exudates away from fovea)  
- Moderate DMO (thickening/exudates near fovea)  
- Severe DMO (thickening involving fovea)

**Pattern-recognition – single finding that confirms/rules out:**
- **New vessels on the disc (NVD)** → confirms PDR (needs urgent ophthalmology)
- **No microaneurysms** on fundoscopy in a diabetic → virtually rules out DR (unless very early NPDR with undetectable MA)

**Test-by-test interpretation:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Dilated fundoscopy** | Direct visualisation of retina, disc, vessels | Screening and grading | See grading above. Cotton-wool spots = ischaemia; IRMA = severe NPDR |
| **Colour fundus photography** | Standardised images | Record baseline, monitor progression | Same as fundoscopy but objective, allows later comparison |
| **Optical coherence tomography (OCT)** | Cross-section of macula – measures retinal thickness, detects fluid | Gold standard for diagnosing DMO | Central subfield thickness >250 µm (or >2 SD above normal) = DMO. Also shows intraretinal and subretinal fluid |
| **Fluorescein angiography (FFA)** | Dynamic view of retinal circulation: leakage, ischaemic areas, new vessels | Assess ischaemia in severe NPDR/PDR; guide laser | Areas of capillary non-perfusion (dark patches) = ischaemia. Leakage from microaneurysms or new vessels |
| **OCT angiography (OCTA)** | Non-invasive, depth-resolved capillary map | Alternative to FFA; shows microaneurysms and non-perfusion | No need for dye; better visualisation of deep capillary plexus |
| **HbA1c** | 2–3 month glycaemic control | Assess modifiable risk factor | Target <7% (53 mmol/mol) for most; but avoid rapid drops |
| **Blood pressure** | Systolic/diastolic | Assess and treat | Target <130/80 mmHg in diabetes |
| **eGFR and urine ACR** | Kidney function | Screen for nephropathy (strongly associated) | If ACR >3 mg/mmol or eGFR <60, higher risk of DR progression |

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour):**  
This applies if you suspect **acute vision-threatening DR** (e.g., sudden visual loss):
1. **Assess visual acuity** and check for relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) – indicates severe ischaemia or retinal detachment.
2. **Check IOP** – neovascular glaucoma can cause high IOP (pain, red eye, cloudy cornea, fixed mid-dilated pupil). If IOP >30 mmHg → urgent ophthalmology referral; may need topical beta-blockers + acetazolamide.
3. **Order B-scan ultrasound** if fundus view is poor (e.g., vitreous haemorrhage). B-scan can confirm retinal detachment or vitreous haemorrhage.
4. **Do NOT dilate if there is any suspicion of neovascular glaucoma** (pain, vomiting, cloudy cornea) – angle closure is contraindication to dilation until IOP controlled.
5. **Immediate ophthalmology referral** for any new PDR, vitreous haemorrhage, or DMO affecting central vision.

**Ward / clinic (24–72 hours):**
1. **Optimise modifiable risk factors:**
   - Glycaemic control: aim for HbA1c <7% but avoid rapid correction (drop <2% per 3 months) – risk of early worsening.
   - BP: target <130/80; start ACEi/ARB (e.g., ramipril 2.5 mg OD, titrate).
   - Lipids: atorvastatin 20–40 mg OD (target LDL <1.8 mmol/L).
   - Smoking cessation advice + nicotine replacement if needed.
2. **Ophthalmology team management:**
   - **Diabetic macular oedema (DMO):** First-line = intravitreal anti-VEGF (ranibizumab 0.5 mg, aflibercept 2 mg, or bevacizumab 1.25 mg). Usually monthly injections for 3–6 months then PRN. If anti-VEGF contraindicated, consider focal/grid laser (less effective).
   - **Proliferative DR:** Panretinal photocoagulation (PRP) – laser burns to peripheral retina to reduce ischaemic drive and regress new vessels. Usually 3–4 sessions.
   - **Vitreous haemorrhage:** If mild, observe for 4–6 weeks; if no clearing, vitrectomy. If severe or with retinal detachment, urgent vitrectomy.
3. **Referral to diabetes team** for structured education and insulin optimisation if needed.

**Long-term:**
1. **Annual retinal screening** – if stable NPDR, repeat per local protocol (typically 12 months). If moderate NPDR, 6–12 monthly.
2. **Continuous risk factor management** – this is where you, as the junior doctor, have the biggest impact. Every clinic visit: check HbA1c, BP, lipids, smoking status.
3. **Aspirin / antiplatelets** – no specific contraindication in DR, but not protective. The ADVANCE and ETDRS trials show no benefit for retinopathy progression.
4. **Avoid:** Use of sildenafil/tadalafil can worsen PDR? Actually, in patients with active PDR, PDE5 inhibitors can cause mild transient visual disturbances but no evidence of harm. No mandatory avoidance – but counsel about risk.

**What NOT to do:**
- Do **not** stop anticoagulation for vitreous haemorrhage unless life-threatening – bleeding is from new vessels, not anticoagulation. Stopping warfarin may cause stroke. Discuss with haematology.
- Do **not** dilate eyes in a diabetic patient with acute painful red eye and hazy cornea – suspect neovascular glaucoma; wait for IOP assessment.
- Do **not** assume “background DR” is safe – any cotton-wool spot or multiple haemorrhages warrants closer follow-up (moderate NPDR can progress to PDR in 20–30% within 1 year).

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Diabetic Retinopathy (NPDR/PDR) | Hypertensive Retinopathy | Retinal Vein Occlusion (CRVO) | Ocular Ischaemic Syndrome |
|---------|----------------------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Laterality** | Bilateral (asymmetric possible) | Bilateral (symmetric) | Usually unilateral | Unilateral (70%) |
| **Pain** | None (unless neovascular glaucoma) | Headache if hypertensive crisis | Dull ache / pressure (uncommon) | Eye pain + periorbital pain; worse with upright posture |
| **Key fundus findings** | Dot/blot haemorrhages, hard exudates, cotton-wool spots, new vessels | Arteriovenous nicking, silver wiring, flame haemorrhages, disc oedema (malignant HTN) | Flame + dot haemorrhages in all quadrants, “blood and thunder” fundus, optic disc oedema, venous tortuosity | Mid-peripheral blot haemorrhages, retinal artery narrowing, cherry-red spot (late), disc neovascularisation |
| **Macular involvement** | DMO common (exudates, thickening) | Macular star (if hypertensive encephalopathy) | Macular oedema common (leading cause of vision loss in CRVO) | Macular oedema less common; cilioretinal artery sparing |
| **Systemic clues** | Past history of diabetes (type 1/2) | High BP, LVH, renal disease | Atherosclerosis, HTN, glaucoma | Carotid bruit, transient ischaemic attacks, stroke—especially ipsilateral |
| **Confirmatory test** | Fundoscopy + OCT; FFA shows leakage and non-perfusion | BP >140/90; fundoscopic grading (Keith-Wagener) | FFA shows delayed arm-to-retina time (CRVO) + blocked background fluorescence | Carotid duplex: >70% stenosis; intraocular pressure normal, but ocular perfusion pressure low |
| **Key differentiator** | Presence of diabetes + symmetric findings; microaneurysms are earliest sign | No microaneurysms; arteriolar changes dominate | Unilateral, abrupt onset, venous tortuosity, and absent diabetes history (though can coexist) | Carotid disease history; fundus shows mid-peripheral haemorrhages but disc neovascularisation (NVD) can mimic PDR |

**Rule of thumb:** If a diabetic patient presents with unilateral fundus changes, think vein occlusion or ocular ischaemic syndrome. Bilateral symmetric NPDR with microaneurysms = DR.

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Vitreous haemorrhage** | Sudden painless “cobwebs” or “curtain” of vision loss, sometimes with flashes (vitreous detachment). Patient may report “seeing black spots” | Fundoscopy: blood visible through dilated pupil (if dense, no view). B-scan: intra-vitreal echoes. RAPD negative unless massive. | Urgent ophthalmology referral. If mild, await spontaneous clearing (weeks). If severe or vision not improving, vitrectomy. Do not give anti-VEGF unless DMO present. |
| **Tractional retinal detachment** | Gradual visual field defect (curtain coming down), often in a patient with known PDR. May follow vitreous haemorrhage | Fundoscopy (if view clear): elevated retina with fibrovascular stalks. B-scan: V-shaped retinal detachment. RAPD often positive if macula off. | Emergency: refer for vitrectomy within 24–48 hours if macula threatened. If macula already off, still urgent (within 1 week). |
| **Neovascular glaucoma** | Red, painful eye with nausea/vomiting, photophobia, cloudy cornea | IOP >30–50 mmHg, conjunctival injection, corneal oedema, mid-dilated pupil (not responding to light), NVI (new vessels on iris) | Emergency: topical beta-blocker (timolol 0.5% BD), acetazolamide 500 mg IV or PO stat. If NVI present, refer immediately for panretinal photocoagulation + anti-VEGF (intravitreal). |
| **Diabetic macular oedema** | Gradual central vision blur, difficulty reading, distortion (metamorphopsia). Often bilateral but asymmetric | Fundoscopy: hard exudates in a ring (circinate) near fovea; OCT shows thickening and cysts. | Refer for anti-VEGF therapy; same-day if visual acuity compromised. Treat risk factors. |
| **Ischaemic optic neuropathy** | Sudden painless loss of vision (altitudinal field defect) in a diabetic (especially in type 1) | RAPD+, disc oedema (normal or pale), no retinal haemorrhages | This is anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (AION) – associated with diabetes but not DR per se. No specific treatment; differentiate from PDR (no neovascularisation). |

**Late-presentation patterns:**  
A patient presents with **sudden, severe, painless loss of vision** in one eye and fundus view is completely obscured by blood (no red reflex). You diagnose **dense vitreous haemorrhage**. The danger is **occult retinal detachment** – if the macula detaches while you wait for clearance, vision may be permanently lost. **Always order B-scan** to check for retinal detachment. If B-scan shows detachment, refer for urgent vitrectomy.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics**

1. **The “4-2-1” rule for severe NPDR:**  
   – 4 quadrants of ≥20 dot/blot haemorrhages  
   – 2 quadrants of venous beading  
   – 1 quadrant of IRMA (intraretinal microvascular abnormalities)  
   *Any one of these = severe NPDR (requires follow-up <6 months, risk of PDR ~50% in 1 year)*

2. **“ABCDES” of DR prevention:**  
   – **A** (HbA1c – “A” for glucose)  
   – **B** (Blood pressure)  
   – **C** (Cholesterol)  
   – **D** (Duration – not modifiable but risk increases)  
   – **E** (Eye screening annually)  
   – **S** (Smoking cessation)

3. **“PDR = New vessels + haemorrhage”** (use to recall the hallmark features)

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **“No symptoms does not mean no disease”** – DR is silent until advanced. Annual screening is the key. The first symptom in PDR is often vitreous haemorrhage (sudden floaters/blur).  
2. **Hard exudates + macular thickening = DMO** – treat with anti-VEGF. Don’t leave it.  
3. **Cotton-wool spots in a diabetic = ischaemic stress** – warrants closer follow-up (every 6 months). But also consider hypertensive retinopathy if BP elevated.  
4. **One eye worse than the other** – think vein occlusion, carotid disease, or media opacity (cataract). Asymmetric DR can occur, but bilateral symmetric is typical.  
5. **Sudden visual loss in a diabetic with known PDR** – assume vitreous haemorrhage until proven otherwise. B-scan first; if no detachment, observe.  
6. **Don’t forget to check the iris** – if you see new vessels on the iris (NVI) or in the angle (gonioscopy), that patient has neovascular glaucoma risk. Panretinal laser is urgent.  
7. **Rapid improvement in HbA1c can worsen DR** – counsel patients: “Slow and steady wins the race.” Aim for gradual reduction (1% per 3 months). If starting intensive insulin therapy (e.g., pump), perform baseline retinal screening and ophthalmology review.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Dilating a patient with neovascular glaucoma (risk of angle closure).  
- Confirming “no retinopathy” based on undilated fundoscopy (you miss peripheral lesions).  
- Missing macular oedema on clinical exam (OCT is superior).  
- Assuming that a single cotton-wool spot in a diabetic is due to DR – could be HIV retinopathy, TIA, or emboli. Check for hypertension and carotid disease.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

| When to escalate | Who to contact | Why | Specific thresholds |
|------------------|----------------|-----|---------------------|
| **New visual symptoms** (floaters, flashes, curtain) in a patient with known DR | Ophthalmology (on-call) | Possible vitreous haemorrhage, retinal tear, or detachment | If visual acuity drops to counting fingers or worse, or if RAPD+ |
| **Acute red painful eye** with cloudy cornea or nausea/vomiting | Ophthalmology (urgent) + call for IOP check | Neovascular glaucoma – vision can be lost in hours | IOP >30 mmHg; treat as emergency |
| **Any new diagnosis of proliferative DR** on screening | Ophthalmology (refer within 1–2 weeks for PRP/anti-VEGF) | High risk of vitreous haemorrhage or retinal detachment | NVD/NVE on any examination |
| **DMO involving fovea** (central subfield thickness >400 µm or VA <6/12) | Ophthalmology (same-day if VA dropped) | Rapid vision loss if untreated | Refer same-week; treat urgently |
| **Systemic deterioration** – diabetic ketoacidosis or hyperosmolar state in a patient with known DR | ICU / medical registrar | Acute metabolic disturbance can worsen retinal perfusion and precipitate macular oedema | If pH <7.2 or glucose >40 mmol/L with ketosis; NEWS2 triggers |
| **NEWS2 ≥7 or any red flag** (hypotension, altered consciousness) | Crash team / ICU | Sepsis, stroke, or other precipitant may cause DR progression | NEWS2 ≥7 triggers immediate OOH immediate review |
| **B-scan shows retinal detachment** (any) | Ophthalmology (emergency) | Risk of permanent vision loss if macula detaches | If macula attached: emergency vitrectomy within 24h. If detached: still urgent. |
| **Asymmetric DR with carotid bruit** | Vascular surgery / stroke team | Ocular ischaemic syndrome may require carotid endarterectomy | If >70% stenosis on duplex, refer for vascular surgery |

**Final thought:** As the junior doctor, you are the gatekeeper. Never ignore a patient’s complaint of visual symptoms, even if their last screening was normal. The retinal signs you miss today can lead to irreversible blindness tomorrow. Document vision, fundoscopy, and IOP in every diabetic admission—it takes
